Journal.Title: Journal of thoracic oncology : official publication of the International Association for the Study of Lung Cancer

Article.ArticleTitle: Long-Term Safety and Effectiveness of Durvalumab in Unresectable Stage III NSCLC in Japan: A Multicenter Prospective Study (AYAME).

Abstract.AbstractText: Based on the PACIFIC trial results, durvalumab after chemoradiotherapy (CRT) is the standard of care for unresectable stage III NSCLC. However, few large-scale studies have prospectively evaluated its long-term effectiveness and safety, including subsequent treatment.

This multicenter, noninterventional study (AYAME) enrolled patients who were prescribed durvalumab for unresectable stage III NSCLC after CRT (July 2019-December 2020; 52 sites; Japan). Patients received durvalumab for a maximum of 12 months and were prospectively followed up for 3 years, including the post-durvalumab treatment period. Primary end points were real-world progression-free survival (rwPFS), the incidence of interstitial lung disease (ILD), and adverse events of special interest (AESIs).

Of the 529 enrolled patients, 512 received durvalumab and 511 comprised the safety analysis population. The median time to onset of the first presentation or occurrence of ILD was 45.0 days. ILD of any grade occurred in 387 patients (75.7%) over the 3-year study period: grade 3, 57 (11.2%); grade 4, none (0.0%); and grade 5, 9 (1.8%). The most common AESIs of any grade during durvalumab treatment were thyroid dysfunction (12.5%), hepatic dysfunction (7.2%), and colitis (2.0%). No durvalumab treatment-related ILD or AESIs occurred during the post-treatment period. The median [95% confidence interval] rwPFS was 23.2 [18.2, 27.2] months in the effectiveness analysis population (n = 495). rwPFS rates were 62.5%, 54.6%, 49.4%, and 40.4% at 12, 18, 24, and 36 months, respectively.
This first and largest prospective, observational study demonstrated the long-term safety and effectiveness of durvalumab for unresectable stage III NSCLC after CRT in a real-world clinical setting.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Disclosure Dr. Kenmotsu has received grants for his institution from Ono Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Novartis Pharma K.K., Eli Lilly K.K., AstraZeneca K.K., and Loxo Oncology, and personal fees from Amgen Inc., AstraZeneca K.K., Bayer, Boehringer Ingelheim, Bristol Myers Squibb, Chugai Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Daiichi Sankyo Co., Ltd., Eli Lilly K.K., Kyowa Hakko Kirin Co., Ltd., Merck, MSD, Novartis Pharma K.K., Ono Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Pfizer, Taiho Pharma, and Takeda Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., in the past 36 months. Dr. Saito has received support for the present manuscript from AstraZeneca K.K. since the initial planning of the work and payment or honoraria from AstraZeneca K.K., MSD K.K., Daiichi Sankyo Co., Ltd., Nippon Boehringer Ingelheim Co., Ltd., Teijin Healthcare Limited, and Insmed Inc. in the past 36 months. Dr. Ninomiya has received personal fees from AstraZeneca, Boehringer Ingelheim, Kyowa Kirin, Eli Lilly Japan, Chugai Pharmaceutical, Nippon Kayaku, Taiho Pharmaceutical, MSD K.K., Ono Pharmaceutical, Takeda Pharmaceutical, Pfizer Inc., Bristol Myers Squibb, Elekta K.K., Janssen Pharma, Daiichi Sankyo, and Amgen K.K. in the past 36 months. Dr. Uematsu, Ms. Fukui, Mr. Koto, Dr. Fujiwara, Dr. Iwao, Dr. Kitagawa, and Dr. Akdemir are employees of AstraZeneca K.K. Dr. Yoshino has received personal fees from AstraZeneca, Covidien Japan, Takeda, Chugai Pharmaceutical, and Johnson & Johnson in the past 36 months. Dr. Gemma has received support for the present manuscript from Chugai since the initial planning of the work; payment or honoraria from Chugai and Nippon Kayaku in the past 36 months; and personal fees for participating in a Data Safety Monitoring Board or an Advisory Board from Daiichi Sankyo and AstraZeneca. Dr. Mitsudomi has received funding for the present manuscript from AstraZeneca since the initial planning of the work and personal fees from AstraZeneca in the past 36 months. Dr. Yamamoto has received support for the present manuscript from AstraZeneca K.K. since the initial planning of the work; grants for his institution from A2 Healthcare Corporation, Amgen K.K., AstraZeneca K.K., Eli Lilly Japan K.K., Mebix, Inc., AbbVie GK, Janssen Pharmaceutical K.K., Bristol Myers Squibb, RPM Co., Ltd., Pfizer R&D Japan G.K., Chugai Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., MSD K.K., Nippon Boehringer Ingelheim Co., Ltd., PRiME-R, Inc., IQVIA Solutions Japan G.K., Novartis Japan, EPS Corporation, Medpace Japan K.K., and Syneos Health Clinical K.K. in the past 36 months; fees for participation on a Data Safety Monitoring Board or Advisory Board from MSD K.K., Amgen K.K., Daiichi Sankyo, Inc., USACO Corporation, AstraZeneca K.K., Ono Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., and Novartis Japan in the past 36 months; and payment or honoraria for self from Amgen K.K., AstraZeneca K.K., Chugai Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., AbbVie GK, Janssen Pharmaceutical K.K., Accuray Japan K.K., Guardant Health Japan Corp., Taiho Oncology, Inc., Tsumura & Co., Nippon Kayaku Co., Ltd., Merck Biopharma Co., Ltd., Otsuka Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Miyarisan Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Kyowa Kirin Co., Ltd., ShiftZero K.K., MSD K.K., Nippon Boehringer Ingelheim Co., Ltd., Eli Lilly Japan K.K., Novartis Japan, Bristol Myers Squibb, Ono Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Daiichi Sankyo, Inc., Takeda Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Terumo Corporation, Pfizer Japan Inc., USACO Corporation, Kyorin Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., Medical Review Co., Ltd., and Kowa Company Ltd. in the past 36 months.
